name: Phoenix, Arizona
tagline: The 5th-largest U.S. city and Arizona's capital — Phoenix runs city technology through its Information Technology Services Department (ITS), backs a balanced FY2026-27 budget on a ~$2.29B General Fund and an $11.4B five-year capital plan, and is an active smart-city and responsible-AI adopter across traffic, water, and resident services.
overview: The City of Phoenix is the government of the fifth-largest city in the United States and the capital of Arizona, serving roughly 1.6 million residents across one of the nation's fastest-growing metros. City technology is run by the Information Technology Services Department (ITS), which delivers citywide IT under a federated model — supporting major operating departments including Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport, Water Services, Police, and Fire alongside general-government services. The city's FY2026-27 budget is balanced, built on a General Fund of about $2.29 billion and supported by a preliminary five-year Capital Improvement Program (CIP) totaling $11.4 billion. Phoenix is a recognized smart-city and responsible-AI adopter: it operates an Office of Innovation, has published a Code of Conduct for the responsible use of generative AI, and has run AI-enabled pilots spanning traffic-signal optimization, water-system monitoring, and energy-efficient building systems. For a technology seller this is a large, growth-driven municipal IT buyer with stable IT leadership (CIO Steen Hambric), a newer CISO (Mitch Kohlbecker, 2025), and a returning City Manager (Ed Zuercher, 2025). Phoenix buys through its own central procurement — vendor registration via procurePHX and solicitations through its OpenGov-based Procurement Portal — and leverages cooperative purchasing under its city code.

budget.intro: Phoenix adopted a balanced FY2026-27 budget anchored by a General Fund of roughly $2.29 billion (up about 4.5% year over year), with a one-time surplus of about $162.5M directed to priorities such as housing, homelessness services, child-care affordability, and employee compensation. Technology spending is embedded across ITS operations and the capital program rather than reported as a single headline line — the preliminary five-year CIP totals $11.4 billion, with the first year alone at about $2.7 billion.

stats.label: General Fund (FY26-27)
stats.note: Balanced adopted budget; ~4.5% increase over the prior year's General Fund

stats.label: 5-year CIP
stats.note: Preliminary five-year Capital Improvement Program; first year ~$2.7B — the pool infrastructure and technology capital draw from

stats.label: FY26-27 surplus
stats.note: One-time surplus directed to housing, homelessness, child care, and compensation in the proposed budget

notes: Phoenix does not publish a single citywide 'IT budget' headline; technology funding flows through ITS operations plus department and CIP capital — confirm the specific funding source before forecasting a given program.

Large operating departments (Aviation/Sky Harbor, Water Services, Police, Fire) carry significant technology demand under the federated IT model — material refresh and modernization opportunity sits inside those departments, not only central ITS.

initiatives.name: Responsible / generative AI program
initiatives.summary: Phoenix has published a Code of Conduct for the Responsible Use of Generative AI and routes approved Gen AI uses through a technical review and committee-approval process. AI is being applied operationally — including an AI-assisted traffic-signal pilot across roughly 30 intersections and a Water Services pilot using AI sensors to monitor sewer pollutants. A live lane for governed AI infrastructure, data platforms, and use-case enablement.

initiatives.name: Smart-city and Office of Innovation pilots
initiatives.summary: The Office of Innovation (stood up in late 2021) co-creates pilots to modernize city processes, and Phoenix has been recognized as an IDC Smart Cities North America Awards finalist (2025). Work spans connected infrastructure, resident-engagement, and sustainability use cases — a recurring entry point for IoT, edge, and data-platform conversations.

initiatives.name: Federated IT modernization across major departments
initiatives.summary: ITS supports a federated model spanning Sky Harbor Airport, Water Services, Police, and Fire. Infrastructure refresh, network, end-user compute, and managed services demand is distributed across these large operating departments as well as central ITS — position ISG and services against department-level capital, not only the central budget.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity under a maturing CISO office
initiatives.summary: With Mitch Kohlbecker promoted to CISO in 2025 (after nearly five years as deputy CISO), the security office is advancing risk management, compliance, and system/security architecture. Active appetite for endpoint, network security, identity, and risk-visibility tooling across a large, multi-department estate.

procurement.intro: Phoenix buys two main ways, and a seller should confirm both. It runs its own central procurement — vendor registration through procurePHX and competitive solicitations through its OpenGov-based Procurement Portal (in use for new solicitations since April 2025) — and it leverages cooperative purchasing authorized under the Phoenix City Code. Confirm Dell's standing in procurePHX and which cooperative vehicles the city actively buys technology through for a given category.

vehicles.name: procurePHX vendor registration
vehicles.detail: The city's vendor registration application, tied to its financial system. Any awarded vendor or payee must be registered in procurePHX — confirm Dell's registration and the relevant commodity categories before pursuing city-specific buys.

vehicles.name: Phoenix Procurement Portal (OpenGov)
vehicles.detail: The city publishes competitive solicitations (RFP/RFQ/IFB) and receives offers through its OpenGov-powered Procurement Portal, used for new solicitations since April 2025. The path for city-specific competitive technology procurements.

vehicles.name: Cooperative purchasing
vehicles.detail: Phoenix City Code authorizes cooperative procurement, allowing the city to buy off pre-competed contracts without a separate RFP. Confirm which national/cooperative vehicles Dell holds and which the city actively uses for hardware, software, and services in the target category.

powerStructure.name: Ed Zuercher
powerStructure.title: City Manager
powerStructure.note: Returned as City Manager in November 2025 (previously City Manager 2013-2021), succeeding the retiring Jeff Barton. Leads city operations, budget, and contracts — the top of the house and owner of budget priorities.

powerStructure.name: Steen Hambric
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (ITS)
powerStructure.note: CIO since 2021, leading the Information Technology Services department and federated IT across Sky Harbor, Water Services, Police, and Fire. Background: ~18 years in IT leadership with the U.S. House of Representatives. The primary citywide IT relationship.

powerStructure.name: Mitch Kohlbecker
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer
powerStructure.note: Promoted to CISO in February 2025 after nearly five years as deputy CISO; succeeded Shannon Lawson (departed for the private sector, later San Antonio CISO). Owns the city's security strategy, risk, and compliance — the lead for security-tooling motions.

powerStructure.name: Michael Hammett
powerStructure.title: Chief Innovation Officer
powerStructure.note: Phoenix's first Chief Innovation Officer (appointed 2021), leading the Office of Innovation, performance/dashboard programs, and smart-city pilots. The relationship for AI, smart-city, and resident-experience use cases.

news.title: Phoenix City Council approves balanced FY2026-27 budget on a ~$2.29B General Fund
news.impact: Stable, surplus-backed budget with an $11.4B five-year CIP. Confirms multi-year infrastructure and capital spend capacity — an entry point for ISG, security, and services across central ITS and major departments.

news.title: Phoenix expands AI-assisted traffic-signal management across ~30 intersections
news.impact: Operational AI moving from pilot toward scale signals appetite for governed AI/edge/data infrastructure beyond back-office use — a credibility-forward smart-city reference lane.

news.title: Ed Zuercher returns as Phoenix City Manager as Jeff Barton retires
news.impact: Leadership change at the top of the house. A returning, experienced City Manager resets executive priorities — align account messaging to the new administration's budget and modernization agenda.

news.title: Mitch Kohlbecker promoted to City of Phoenix CISO
news.impact: Internal security leader (ex-deputy CISO) now owns the city's security agenda. Build the CISO relationship for endpoint, network, identity, and risk-tooling across the multi-department estate.

news.title: Phoenix named a finalist in IDC's 2025 Smart Cities North America Awards
news.impact: Reinforces Phoenix's smart-city credibility and active pilot pipeline through the Office of Innovation — useful context for positioning IoT, data, and connected-infrastructure offers.

dellActions: Anchor the account on stable IT leadership — CIO Steen Hambric (since 2021) and CISO Mitch Kohlbecker (2025) — and align messaging to returning City Manager Ed Zuercher's FY2026-27 budget and modernization priorities.

Sell into the federated IT model: pursue ISG, network, end-user compute, and managed-services refresh inside the large operating departments (Sky Harbor, Water Services, Police, Fire) as well as central ITS, mapped against the $11.4B five-year CIP.

Lead AI infrastructure and data-platform conversations through the Office of Innovation and the city's responsible/generative-AI program — Phoenix's published AI Code of Conduct and live traffic/water pilots make a governed-AI motion credible.
Bring municipal security offers (endpoint, network, identity, risk visibility) tied to the maturing CISO office under Mitch Kohlbecker, framed for a large multi-department estate.

Confirm Dell's procurePHX vendor registration and standing in the city's OpenGov Procurement Portal, and verify which cooperative purchasing vehicles the city actively uses so catalog and competitive buys both have a clear path.
